e: Hlava (pronounced 'hlah-vah') is the Czech word for 'head' - the part of the body containing the brain. Used in the example: 'Jeho hlava byla zraněna.' (His head was injured.)
Bolest hlavy literally means 'head pain' or 'pain of the head' and refers to a headache. From the example: 'Já mám bolesti hlavy od minulého týdne.' (I have had a headache since last week.)
Stres (pronounced 'stres') means to be stressed or stressed out. From the example: 'Byl jsem vystresovaný.' (I was stressed.)
Horečka (pronounced 'hoh-rech-kah') is the Czech word for fever - an elevated body temperature. Example: 'On měl horečku tři dny.' (He had a fever for three days.)

Pacient (pronounced 'pah-tsee-ent') is the Czech word for patient - a person receiving medical treatment. From example: 'Pacient začal mít včera v noci v 19h bolesti hlavy.' (The patient started having a headache last night at 7 PM.)
Zub (pronounced 'zoop') is the Czech word for tooth. It's one of the hard structures in the mouth used for biting and chewing food.

Bolest zubů (pronounced 'boh-lest zoo-boo') literally means 'tooth pain' or 'pain of the teeth' and refers to a toothache. Example: 'Od dnešního rána ho bolí zuby.' (His teeth have been hurting since this morning.)

Dáseň (pronounced 'dah-sen') is the Czech word for gums - the soft tissue in the mouth that surrounds and supports the teeth. From example: 'Již několik dnů jí bolí dásně.' (Her gums have been hurting for several days.)

Zubař (pronounced 'zoo-bahrsh') is the Czech word for dentist - a medical professional who specializes in treating teeth and oral health. From example: 'Zubní lékař mě žádá, abych si lehl.' (The dentist is asking me to lie down.)
Otevřít ústa (pronounced 'oh-tev-sheet oos-tah') literally means 'to open one's mouth'. Example: 'Zubní lékař mě žádá, abych otevřel ústa.' (The dentist is asking me to open my mouth.)
Zkontrolovat (pronounced 'skohn-troh-loh-vaht') means to check or verify. From example: 'Já požádám zubaře, aby mi zkontroloval zuby.' (I will ask the dentist to check my teeth.)
Žaludek (pronounced 'zhah-loo-dek') is the Czech word for stomach - the organ that digests food. It's a common body part discussed in medical contexts.
Bolení břicha literally means 'belly/abdomen pain' and refers to a stomachache. From example: 'Ona má od včerejška bolesti žaludku.' (She has had stomach pain since yesterday.)
Průjem (pronounced 'proo-yem') is the Czech word for diarrhea - a digestive condition. From example: 'Možná máte průjem.' (Maybe you have diarrhea.)

Otrava jídlem (pronounced 'oh-trah-vah yee-dlem') literally means 'food poisoning' - an illness from eating contaminated food. From example: 'Možná máte otravu jídlem.' (Maybe you have food poisoning.)
Být zkažený (pronounced 'boot zkah-zhen-ee') means to be spoiled or gone bad, referring to food that has deteriorated. From example: 'Možná bylo vaše jídlo zkažené.' (Maybe your food was spoiled.)
Příznak (pronounced 'pree-znawk') is the Czech word for symptom - a sign of illness or disease. From example: 'Příznaky se zhoršují.' (The symptoms are getting worse.)
Doktor (pronounced 'dohk-tor') is the Czech word for doctor - a medical professional. From example: 'On pracuje jako lékař od roku 2000.' (He has been working as a doctor since 2000.)
Navštívit lékaře (pronounced 'nah-shtee-vit lek-ah-rzhe') means to visit or see the doctor. From example: 'Já potřebuji navštívit doktora.' (I need to see the doctor.)

q: What is a 'zdravotní sestřička' in English?
e: Zdravotní sestřička (pronounced 'zdrah-voh-nee ses-trzhitch-kah') is the Czech word for nurse - a medical professional who cares for patients. From example: 'Pacient potřebuje mluvit se zdravotní sestrou.' (The patient needs to talk to the nurse.)

Nemocnice (pronounced 'neh-mohts-nee-tseh') is the Czech word for hospital - a medical facility for treating patients. From example: 'Musím jít do nemocnice a navštívit doktora.' (I have to go to the hospital and see the doctor.)
Léky (pronounced 'lek-ee') is the Czech word for medicine or medicines - substances used to treat illness. From example: 'Já potřebuji koupit léky.' (I need to buy medicine.)

Lékárna (pronounced 'lek-ahr-nah') is the Czech word for pharmacy - a place where medicine is sold. From example: 'Já musím jít do lékárny, abych koupil léky.' (I have to go to the pharmacy to buy medicine.)
Hnědý (pronounced 'hneh-dee') is the Czech word for the color brown. From example: 'Kočka je hnědá.' (The cat is brown.)
Oči (pronounced 'oh-chee') is the Czech word for eyes - the organs of sight. From example: 'Ona má hnědé oči.' (She has brown eyes.)
Obličej (pronounced 'oh-blih-chay') is the Czech word for face - the front part of the head. From example: 'Jeho obličej je kulatý jako fotbalový míč.' (His face is round like a soccer ball.)
Vlasy (pronounced 'vlah-see') is the Czech word for hair - the strands growing from the head. From example: 'Její vlasy jsou jemné jako hedvábí.' (Her hair is fine like silk.)
Dítě (pronounced 'dyee-teh') is the Czech word for baby - a very young human. From example: 'To dítě má modré oči.' (That baby has blue eyes.)
Porovnat (pronounced 'poh-roh-vnaht') means to compare or to compare to. From example: 'Ve srovnání s tou ženou, jsi velmi krásná.' (Compared to that woman, you are very beautiful.)

Silný (pronounced 'sil-nee') is the Czech word for strong - having great physical power. From example: 'Ve srovnání s jeho přítelem, on vypadá velmi silně.' (Compared to his friend, he looks very strong.)

Hubený (pronounced 'hoo-beh-nee') is an adjective, though the translation 'cute' in the source seems to suggest an affectionate term for someone who is slim or petite. From example: 'Ve srovnání s tou ženou jsem hubený.' (Compared to that woman, I am slim.)
Mladý (pronounced 'mlah-dee') is the Czech word for young - not old in age. From example: 'Ve srovnání s jejím věkem, vypadá velmi mladě.' (Compared to her age, she looks very young.)
Tělo (pronounced 'teh-loh') is the Czech word for body - the physical form of a person. From example: 'Ona je hubená, ale její tělo je velmi silné.' (She is slim, but her body is very strong.)
Sval (pronounced 'svahl') is the Czech word for muscle - tissue that enables movement. From example: 'Jeho svaly vypadají větší než dříve.' (His muscles look bigger than before.)
Kudrnatý (pronounced 'koo-dr-nah-tee') is the Czech word for curly - having curls or waves. From example: 'Její vlasy jsou kudrnatější než předtím.' (Her hair is curlier than before.)
Vlevo (pronounced 'vleh-voh') is the Czech word for left - the direction opposite to right. From example: 'On je levák.' (He is left-handed.)

Temný (pronounced 'tem-nee') is the Czech word for dark - lacking light or having a dark color. From example: 'Její pokožka je tmavší a jemnější než dříve.' (Her skin is darker and more delicate than before.)

Pravý (pronounced 'prah-vee') is the Czech word for right - the direction opposite to left. From example: 'Jeho pravá paže je silnější než jeho levá paže.' (His right arm is stronger than his left arm.)
Vzhled (pronounced 'vzhled') is the Czech word for appearance - how someone or something looks. It refers to physical appearance or aesthetic qualities.
Popsat (pronounced 'pohp-saht') is the Czech word for to describe - to give an account of something in words. From example: 'Ona popisuje jeho vzhled.' (She describes his appearance.)
Modrý (pronounced 'moh-dree') is the Czech word for the color blue. From example: 'Ten muž, který má modré oči, je můj přítel.' (The man who has blue eyes is my friend.)

Legrační (pronounced 'leg-rah-tsi-nee') is the Czech word for funny - amusing or humorous. From example: 'Ten muž, který má kulatý obličej, je velmi zábavný.' (The man with the round face is very funny.)
Chytrý (pronounced 'khee-tree') is the Czech word for smart - intelligent or mentally quick. From example: 'Ten chlapec se zlomenou levou paží je chytrý.' (The boy with the broken left arm is smart.)

Sexy (pronounced 'sek-see') is a borrowed English word used in Czech to describe someone as sexually attractive or appealing. From example: 'Ta žena, která má tmavou kůži a dlouhé kudrnaté vlasy, je velmi sexy.' (The woman with dark skin and long curly hair is very sexy.)
Počasí (pronounced 'poh-chah-see') is the Czech word for weather - the state of the atmosphere. From example: 'Dnes je pěkné počasí.' (Today is nice weather.)
Chladný (pronounced 'khlad-nee') is the Czech word for cool or cold - having a low temperature. From example: 'Dnes je chladno.' (Today is cold.)
Slunný (pronounced 'sloon-nee') is the Czech word for sunny - characterized by sunshine. From example: 'Je slunečno.' (It is sunny.)
Větrný (pronounced 'veh-tr-nee') is the Czech word for windy - characterized by wind. From example: 'Je větrno.' (It is windy.)
Mrznout (pronounced 'mrz-noh-oot') is the Czech word for freezing - turning to ice or very cold. From example: 'Dnes bude mrznout.' (It will freeze today.)
Pršet (pronounced 'prshet') is the Czech word for to rain - water falling from clouds. From example: 'Prší.' (It is raining.)

q: What does the Czech word 'déšť' mean?
e: Déšť is the Czech noun for rain. It commonly appears in weather-related sentences like 'Bude pršet' (It will rain).
Sněžit is a verb meaning to snow. Used to describe the action of snowfall, as in 'Bude sněžit' (It will snow).
Přestat means to stop or cease an action. Example: 'Déšť brzy přestane' (The rain will soon stop).

q: What is the Czech word for snow?
e: Sníh is the Czech noun for snow, a frozen form of precipitation. Often used in winter weather contexts.
Tajfun refers to a typhoon, a powerful tropical cyclone common in Southeast Asia. Czech: 'Vypadá to, že přijde tajfun' (It looks like a typhoon is coming).
Bouřka is the Czech word for storm, a severe weather phenomenon. Used in 'Vypadá to, že přijde bouře' (It looks like a storm is coming).

q: What is the Czech word for temperature?
e: Teplota is the Czech noun for temperature, measuring heat levels in degrees. Essential for weather discussions.
Nízký is an adjective meaning low, used to describe height, position, or levels. Example: 'Teplota je poměrně nízká' (The temperature is quite low).
Stoupat means to rise or go up. Often used for temperatures or levels increasing: 'teplota stoupá' (temperature is rising).
Silně is an adverb meaning heavily or intensely. Used in contexts like heavy rain: 'silně pršet' (to rain heavily).
Předpovídat means to forecast or predict future events. Often used for weather predictions: 'předpověď deště' (rain forecast).

q: What does 'být mlhavo' mean in Czech?
e: Být mlhavo means to be foggy, describing weather conditions with low visibility due to fog. Used in weather descriptions.
Sněhová bouře refers to a snowstorm, a severe winter weather event with heavy snow and strong winds. Example: 'Zítra může být sněhová bouře' (There might be a snowstorm tomorrow).

q: What does 'být zataženo' mean in Czech?
e: Být zataženo means to be cloudy or overcast. Used in weather forecasts: 'bude celý den zataženo' (it will be cloudy all day).
Prášek is the Czech word for pill, a medication form. Example: 'Vezme prášek, protože má bolesti hlavy' (He takes a pill because he has a headache).
Nemocný is an adjective meaning sick or ill. Used to describe someone with illness: 'je nemocná' (she is sick).
Unavený is an adjective meaning tired or fatigued. Example: 'Já jsem opravdu unavený' (I am really tired).
Žíznivý is an adjective meaning thirsty or needing water. Example: 'je žíznivý' (he is thirsty).
Potit se is a reflexive verb meaning to sweat. Often caused by heat or fever: 'On se potí, protože jeho tělesná teplota je velmi vysoká' (He sweats because his body temperature is very high).

q: What is the Czech word for reason?
e: Důvod is the Czech noun for reason or cause. Used to explain the rationale for something: 'Jaký je důvod?' (What is the reason?).
Předpis is the Czech word for prescription, a doctor's written order for medicine. Example: 'Lékař napsal předpis pro pacienta' (The doctor wrote a prescription for the patient).
Vařit is a verb meaning to cook or boil. Used both for cooking meals and for boiling water. Example: 'Ona pro něj vařila' (She cooked for him).
Kytice is the Czech word for a bouquet, a collection of flowers. Often given as a gift: 'On jí přinesl kytici' (He brought her a bouquet).

q: What is the Czech word for present (gift)?
e: Dárek is the Czech noun for a present or gift. Example: 'Udělala jsem pro ní tento dárek' (I made this present for her).
Lék is the Czech word for medicine or medication used to treat illness. Example: 'Já koupím léky v lékarně' (I will buy medicines at the pharmacy).

q: What is the Czech word for umbrella?
e: Deštník is the Czech noun for an umbrella, used for protection from rain. Example: 'Prší, takže používáme deštníky' (It's raining, so we use umbrellas).
Klimatizace is the Czech word for air-conditioning or air-conditioner. Used to cool indoor spaces: 'Je horko, a tak jsme zapnuli klimatizaci' (It's hot, so we turned on the air-conditioner).
Topení is the Czech word for heating or a heater system used to warm spaces. Example: 'Je zima, a tak jsem zapnula topení' (It's cold, so I turned on the heating).

q: What is the Czech word for window?
e: Okno is the Czech noun for window, an opening in a wall. Example: 'On mi pomohl umýt okna' (He helped me wash the windows).
Bolest is the Czech noun for pain or ache. Example: 'Bolela ho záda' (His back hurt).
Krásný is an adjective meaning beautiful or lovely. Example: 'Šátek je tak krásný' (The scarf is so beautiful).
Pozdě is an adverb meaning late or after the expected time. Example: 'On vstal tak pozdě, že nestihl autobus' (He woke up so late that he missed the bus).
Zrušit means to cancel or abolish something planned. Example: 'Pršelo tak silně, že jsem zrušil schůzku' (It rained so heavily that I canceled the meeting).

q: What does 'jít ven' mean in Czech?
e: Jít ven means to go out or go outside. A common phrase for outdoor activities. Example: 'Chtěl bys se mnou jít ven?' (Would you like to go out with me?).
Připojit se is a reflexive verb meaning to join or become part of a group. Example: 'Chcete se připojit?' (Do you want to join?).
Pozvat means to invite someone to an event or gathering. Example: 'Chtěl bych vás pozvat na kávu' (I'd like to invite you for coffee).
Šance is the Czech word for chance or opportunity. Example: 'Já mu dám ještě jednu šanci' (I'll give him one more chance).

q: What is the Czech word for time?
e: Čas is the Czech noun for time, referring to duration or a point in time. Example: 'Já nebudu mít čas' (I won't have time).

s: být k dispozici = to be available
q: What does 'být k dispozici' mean in Czech?
e: Být k dispozici means to be available or at someone's disposal. Example: 'Já budu k dispozici dnes večer' (I will be available tonight).
Opravdu is an adverb meaning really or truly. Used for emphasis: 'Já jsem opravdu šťastný' (I am really happy).
Zaneprázdněný is an adjective meaning busy or occupied with tasks. Example: 'Já jsem opravdu zaneprázdněn' (I am really busy).

s: být si jistý = to be sure
e: Být si jistý means to be sure or certain about something. Example: 'Já si opravdu nejsem jistý' (I am really not sure).
Znuděný is an adjective meaning bored or lacking interest. Example: 'Já jsem opravdu znuděný' (I am really bored).
Anděl is the Czech word for angel, a celestial being. Often used metaphorically: 'Ty jsi velmi krásná jako anděl' (You are as beautiful as an angel).
Pohledný is an adjective meaning handsome, typically used for men. Example: 'On je pohledný jako jeho otec' (He is handsome like his father).
Dobře vypadající means good looking or attractive in appearance. A more general term than specific adjectives. Example: 'On vypadá dobře' (He looks good).
Okouzlující is an adjective meaning charming or enchanting. Example: 'On je okouzlující muž' (He is a charming man).

q: What is the Czech word for doll?
e: Panenka is the Czech noun for a doll, a toy figure representing a person. Often used in playful comparisons.
Roztomilý is an adjective meaning cute or adorable. Example: 'Ona je roztomilá jako panenka' (She is as cute as a doll).
Úsměv is the Czech noun for a smile, a facial expression showing happiness. Example: 'Líbí se mi tvůj úsměv' (I like your smile).
